subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods — Correlation, Perception, and Social Influence
stimulus: Dr. Knowles is interested in exploring the relationship between garment cost and perceptions of clothing quality. She predicts that higher-priced clothing will be perceived as being of higher quality. She creates a Web site that presents floral shirts listed at a variety of prices. Participants are directed to the Web site, where they are asked to rate the quality of each shirt (on a 1 to 10 scale, with higher numbers meaning better quality). The data collected are presented in the scatterplot.

parts.prompt: State the hypothesis that Dr. Knowles tested in the study.

scoring_points.description: The response must state the hypothesis naming both variables AND indicating the direction of the predicted relationship (positive/direct). Simply saying there is 'a correlation between price and quality' without stating the direction does not earn the point.

required_elements: Names both variables (price and perceived quality) with the correct direction (positive/direct — higher price = higher perceived quality).
alternatives.correct_example: Dr. Knowles hypothesized that higher-priced clothing would be perceived as being of higher quality — a positive correlation between price and perceived quality.

wrong_examples: Dr. Knowles hypothesized there would be a correlation between price and quality.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must state Dr. Knowles's hypothesis by naming both variables (garment price and perceived quality) AND indicating the direction — a positive/direct relationship (higher price → higher perceived quality). Responses that name a 'correlation' without specifying its direction do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Dr. Knowles hypothesized that higher-priced clothing would be perceived as being of higher quality — that price and perceived quality would have a positive correlation.
sample_responses.commentary: Names both variables and correctly states the predicted direction (positive — higher price = higher perceived quality).

sample_responses.response: The perceived quality of the shirts is positively correlated with price.
sample_responses.commentary: Concise hypothesis statement naming both variables with the correct direction (positive/direct).

sample_responses.response: Dr. Knowles hypothesized there would be a correlation between price and quality.
sample_responses.commentary: Names both variables but does not specify the direction of the predicted relationship — 'a correlation' could be positive or negative.

parts.prompt: Identify the operational definition Dr. Knowles used for quality.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that Dr. Knowles operationally defined quality as a score on the 1-to-10 rating scale. Referring to 'the rating' without specifying the numeric scale does not earn the point.

required_elements: Operational definition of quality = score on the 1-to-10 rating scale.
alternatives.correct_example: Dr. Knowles operationally defined quality as a score from 1 to 10.

wrong_examples: The operational definition is the rating of the shirt.
scoring_points.official_rubric: An operational definition specifies how a variable is measured. Dr. Knowles operationally defined 'quality' as participants' numeric score on a 1-to-10 rating scale (higher numbers = better quality). The response must include the specific numeric scale (1 to 10) or clearly reference the numeric rating. Saying 'the rating of the shirt' without specifying the 1-to-10 scale is too vague.

sample_responses.response: Dr. Knowles operationally defined quality as a score from 1 to 10 on the rating scale, with higher numbers indicating better quality.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the operational definition — the specific numeric rating scale used to measure perceived quality.

sample_responses.response: The operational definition of quality is the rating of the shirt.
sample_responses.commentary: Refers to 'the rating' generically without specifying the 1-to-10 numeric scale — too vague to earn the point.

parts.prompt: Identify the type of relationship found between the variables of interest in the study.

scoring_points.description: The response must name the type of relationship using an acceptable term: negative correlation, inverse correlation, negative relationship, or inverse relationship. Describing the direction (as one goes up the other goes down) WITHOUT using an acceptable term does not earn the point.

required_elements: Uses an acceptable term: 'negative correlation,' 'inverse correlation,' 'negative relationship,' or 'inverse relationship.'

wrong_examples: There is a correlation between cost of shirt and perceived quality.
As the cost of the shirts goes up, the perceived quality goes down.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The scatterplot shows that as shirt price increases, perceived quality ratings decrease — a negative (inverse) correlation. The response must use one of the acceptable terms: 'negative correlation,' 'inverse correlation,' 'negative relationship,' or 'inverse relationship.' Describing the direction without using one of these terms (e.g., 'as one goes up the other goes down') does not earn the point. Saying only 'a correlation' without specifying direction does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: The relationship between shirt price and perceived quality is a negative correlation.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses the required term ('negative correlation') to identify the type of relationship shown in the scatterplot.

sample_responses.response: It is an inverse relationship — higher price is associated with lower perceived quality.
sample_responses.commentary: 'Inverse relationship' is an acceptable term for a negative correlation.

sample_responses.response: As the cost of the shirts goes up, the perceived quality goes down.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly describes the direction of the relationship but does not use one of the required terms (negative correlation, inverse correlation, etc.).

sample_responses.response: There is a correlation between the cost of the shirt and perceived quality.
sample_responses.commentary: Names a correlation but does not specify its direction — could be positive or negative.

parts.prompt: Explain why Dr. Knowles cannot generalize the results of her study to the general population.

scoring_points.description: The response must explain why generalization is not possible — either (1) the sample is too small (only ~10 data points visible on the scatterplot), or (2) the sample is not representative of the general population (e.g., self-selected participants interested in floral shirts, no random sampling). The design being correlational rather than experimental is not a valid generalizability reason.

required_elements: Small sample size OR non-representative/biased sample.
alternatives.correct_example: Dr. Knowles can’t generalize because there are only about 10 subjects in the scatterplot — too small a sample to represent the general population.

required_elements: Sample not representative — participants likely self-selected / interested in floral shirts.
alternatives.correct_example: The sample is likely not representative because participants directed to the Web site may already have an interest in floral shirts, which is not typical of the general population.

wrong_examples: Dr. Knowles cannot generalize because she did not conduct a true experiment.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Generalization to a population requires a large, representative (ideally randomly sampled) sample. The response must identify either the small sample size (scatterplot shows ~10 data points) or the non-representative nature of the sample (self-selected participants visiting a floral shirt website, no random sampling). Saying the study can’t generalize because it was correlational rather than experimental is incorrect — correlational studies can generalize if sampling is adequate; the issue here is sample quality/size.

sample_responses.response: Dr. Knowles can’t generalize because the scatterplot shows only about 10 participants — the sample is too small to represent the general population.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the small sample size as the limitation preventing generalization.

sample_responses.response: The study’s results cannot be generalized because participants were self-selected — only people already interested in floral shirts would visit this Web site, making the sample biased and not representative of the general population.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the non-representative (self-selected, biased) nature of the sample as the generalizability limitation.

sample_responses.response: Dr. Knowles cannot generalize because she did not conduct a true experiment.
sample_responses.commentary: Experimental design is not required for generalization — the issue is the sample’s representativeness and size, not the research design.

parts.prompt: Explain how Mere-exposure effect relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that repeated exposure to the shirts or the Web site leads to increased liking of the shirts. Acceptable ways to indicate repetition: referencing number of exposures (seen multiple times) or amount of time spent on the site.

required_elements: Repeated exposure to the shirts/Web site leads to increased liking.
alternatives.correct_example: The more often that people look at the Web site, the more they like the shirts.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The mere-exposure effect holds that repeated exposure to a stimulus leads to increased liking. The response must state that repeated exposure (multiple visits to the site, seeing the shirts many times) leads to increased liking of the floral shirts. Acceptable references to repetition include: number of visits to the site, amount of time spent viewing, or number of times the shirts are seen.

sample_responses.response: The more often people visit the Web site and view the floral shirts, the more they tend to like them — consistent with the mere-exposure effect.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies repeated exposure (multiple visits/viewings) as the cause of increased liking.

sample_responses.response: Participants who spent more time on the Web site grew to like the shirts more than those who visited briefly.
sample_responses.commentary: Amount of time spent is an acceptable indicator of greater exposure, and increased liking is correctly attributed to that greater exposure.

sample_responses.response: Participants liked the shirts because the floral designs were attractive.
sample_responses.commentary: Attributes liking to the shirt's aesthetic quality rather than to the repetition of exposure — does not apply the mere-exposure effect.

parts.prompt: Explain how Cones of the retina relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that cones (photoreceptors in the retina) enable the perception of color or fine visual detail of the floral shirts. Acceptable from either direction: cones help people see the shirts’ colors, OR malfunction of cones would hinder color perception of the shirts.

required_elements: Cones enable color or fine-detail perception of the floral shirts.
alternatives.correct_example: Cones help people see the colors of the floral shirts on the Web site.

required_elements: Malfunction of cones would impair ability to see the shirt colors.
alternatives.correct_example: If a participant’s cones were damaged, they would not be able to see the colorful floral patterns on the shirts clearly.

scoring_points.official_rubric: Cones are photoreceptors in the retina that detect color and fine visual detail; they are concentrated in the fovea and function best in bright light. The response must connect cones to color perception or fine detail in the context of viewing the floral shirts on Dr. Knowles’s Web site. Either direction is acceptable: cones enable color perception, or absence/malfunction of cones would prevent it.

sample_responses.response: Cones in the retina help participants see the colors and floral patterns on the shirts when viewing the Web site.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies cones as enabling color/fine-detail perception and applies it to viewing the floral shirts.

sample_responses.response: If a participant’s cones were not functioning properly, they would be unable to perceive the colors of the floral shirts, affecting their quality rating.
sample_responses.commentary: Applies cones from the negative direction — malfunction impairs color perception of the shirts.

sample_responses.response: Rods in the retina help participants see the shirts in the dark.
sample_responses.commentary: Rods detect low-light/dim conditions but not color. The question asks about cones specifically, and color perception of floral patterns is the relevant application.

parts.prompt: Explain how Prototype relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that participants’ ratings of the shirts are influenced by their mental prototype — their idea of the best, most typical, or ideal floral shirt. Participants compare the shirts on the site to their prototype. Using ‘prototype’ to mean a physical sample garment (product design sense) does not earn the point.

required_elements: Participants’ quality ratings are influenced by their mental prototype (ideal/most typical floral shirt).
alternatives.correct_example: Participants compare their idea of what the ideal floral shirt should look like to the shirts on the Web site, rating shirts higher when they match the prototype.

wrong_examples: Dr. Knowles made a shirt prototype first, then made all the other shirts based on that original design.
scoring_points.official_rubric: In cognitive psychology, a prototype is the mental representation of the most typical or ideal example of a category. When participants rate the floral shirts, they compare each shirt to their prototype of a ‘floral shirt’ — their mental ideal. The response must indicate that the shirts’ quality ratings are influenced by how well they match participants’ prototype (ideal/most typical floral shirt). Using ‘prototype’ in the product-design sense (a physical model) does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Participants compare each floral shirt on the Web site to their prototype — their mental idea of what the ideal floral shirt looks like — and rate the shirts higher when they closely match that prototype.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly defines prototype as a mental ideal/most-typical representation and applies it to the shirt-rating task.

sample_responses.response: If a shirt matches a participant’s mental image of a typical, high-quality floral shirt, they will give it a higher quality rating.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly applies prototype — the mental image of a typical floral shirt serves as the comparison standard for quality ratings.

sample_responses.response: Dr. Knowles made a shirt prototype first, then made all the other shirts based on that original design.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses ‘prototype’ in the product-development/engineering sense (a physical model) rather than the psychological sense (mental representation of the most typical category member).
